title: Rights and liabilities of lessor and lessee — tenant protections
text: Section 108 of the Transfer of Property Act 1882 sets out the rights and liabilities of both the lessor and lessee. The lessor is bound to disclose any material defect in the property. The lessor must put the lessee in possession of the property. The lessee has the right to be put in possession of the property. The lessee may use the property and its produce in a reasonable manner. The lessee shall not commit waste upon the property. The lessee shall not erect any permanent structure without the lessor's consent. The lessee has the right to remove fixtures erected by the lessee provided no damage is done. Critically, the lessee is entitled to the benefit of all improvements made to the property during the lease. The lessor cannot interfere with the lessee's quiet enjoyment of the property — cutting off utilities such as water or electricity, padlocking the premises, or removing the tenant's belongings are all illegal acts amounting to unlawful eviction. The lessee has the right to sue the lessor for damages if the lessor interferes with the lessee's quiet enjoyment of the property. Under Section 108(q), if the lessor neglects to make repairs which he is bound to make, the lessee may make the same himself and deduct the expense from the rent. The lessee is entitled to all accretions to the leased property. These rights exist regardless of whether the lease is written or verbal.
